selected_skills.trigger_context: Use when a PR changes cache behavior, cached reads, mutation paths, update
selected_skills.trait_instruction: Use when a PR changes cache behavior, cached reads, mutation paths, update

focus_areas: Cache invalidation on user update paths
Stale-read risks between database and Redis cache
Behavioral impact of removing cache eviction in src/cache/userCache.ts
Consistency between cached user reads and mutation flows across routes/services
specific_checks: Verify whether user records are cached under keys like `user:${userId}` anywhere in the repository and whether reads depend on eviction after updates.
Confirm whether removing `cache.del(`user:${userId}`)` can cause stale data to be served after `db.users.update(userId, data)`.
Check if there is an alternative cache refresh/write-through mechanism that makes deletion unnecessary; if none exists, flag this as a correctness regression.

Review all user mutation entry points and read paths (e.g. routes/users.ts, routes/profile.ts, middleware/auth.ts, cache/accountCache.ts) for assumptions that updated user data becomes immediately visible.
Recommend tests covering: update user -> subsequent cached read returns fresh data; update failure does not corrupt cache state; repeated updates do not leave stale Redis entries.
risk_profile: hidden correctness regressions in update paths

initial_reviewer_genomes.persona_name: User Data Flow & API Surface Reviewer
focus_areas: Cross-module coupling between db, cache, and route handlers
specific_checks: Inspect src/db/users.ts, src/routes/users.ts, src/routes/profile.ts, and src/middleware/auth.ts to determine whether they read cached user state that would now remain stale.
Check whether any admin/import/auth flows update users indirectly and rely on `updateUser` for consistency.
Assess whether the change alters observable API behavior, especially immediately after profile/account updates.
Suggest targeted regression tests in tests/users.test.ts for end-to-end user update/read scenarios involving cache presence.
risk_profile: authorization decisions based on outdated cached user state

Inspect src/db/users.ts, src/routes/users.ts, src/routes/profile.ts, and src/middleware/auth.ts to determine whether they read cached user state that would now remain stale.

authorization decisions based on outdated cached user state

hypothetical_bugs: Stale read-after-write on any path that reads users via Redis key `user:${userId}`: `updateUser()` now updates the DB but leaves the old cached user intact, so immediate GET /users, GET /profile, auth/session hydration, or middleware lookups may continue returning pre-update fields until TTL/manual eviction.

Authorization or account-state regressions caused by stale cached user records after sensitive updates: if roles, permissions, email verification, disabled/locked status, password/auth flags, or tenant membership are changed in the DB, middleware/auth code that still reads `user:${userId}` from cache may make decisions using outdated state.

Cross-path inconsistency because only this mutation path stopped evicting cache: systems with multiple user update entry points (profile edit, admin update, import/sync, auth-related updates) may now diverge depending on which helper they use, producing hard-to-reproduce bugs where some updates become immediately visible while others leave stale profile/API responses or overwrite newer DB state when later logic reuses cached objects.

This remains a high-severity API/cache behavior regression: cached reads still use `user:${userId}`, but updates no longer invalidate or refresh that entry, allowing

iterations.final_synthesized_review_summary: 1 comments (High:1). This change introduces a high-severity logic regression in `src/cache/userCache.ts`: user updates no longer invalidate or refresh the Redis entry used by `getUser()`, so callers can receive stale data immediately after a
iterations.evaluator_comments: Accurate and well-grounded review: it identifies a concrete regression at a precise location, explains the stale-read impact clearly, and provides actionable fixes plus a useful test suggestion. The only minor gap is that it assumes `getUser()` and `updateUser()` share the exact same cache key and consistency contract; if that is guaranteed by the code, this is essentially complete.
